{"step_by_step_thinking": "To determine which amino acid does not undergo post-translational modification, we analyze each option:

A: Selenocysteine - According to the MEDCORP document [1], selenocysteine is one of the 21st amino acids and is incorporated into proteins during ribosomal synthesis. It is specifically noted that 'these 22 amino acids may also be modified through a post-translational modification to add further diversity in generating proteins.' This indicates that selenocysteine can undergo post-translational modification.

B: Triiodothyronine - This is not an amino acid; it is a thyroid hormone derived from tyrosine. It is not incorporated into proteins and does not undergo post-translational modification in the context of protein synthesis. It is a hormone, not a proteinogenic amino acid. Therefore, it does not fit the category of amino acids that undergo post-translational modification.

C: Hydroxyproline - This is a modified form of proline. It is formed by post-translational hydroxylation of proline in collagen. This is a well-documented post-translational modification.

D: Hydroxylysine - This is a modified form of lysine, formed by post-translational hydroxylation in collagen. This is also a well-documented post-translational modification.

Since the question asks which amino acid does not include post-translational modification, and triiodothyronine is not an amino acid at all (it is a hormone), it does not undergo post-translational modification in the context of amino acid processing. The other options are all amino acids (or derivatives) that are known to undergo post-translational modification.

Thus, the correct answer is B: Triiodothyronine.", "answer_choice": "B"}
